title: Hypoglycemia: Recognition and the Rule of 15
subtitle: A patient on insulin becomes shaky, sweaty, and confused with a low blood glucose. The nurse recognizes hypoglycemia, treats with the Rule of 15, and knows how care changes if the patient cannot swallow.

patient.name: Hypoglycemia
patient.admitDate: A patient on insulin develops shakiness, sweating, and confusion with a low blood glucose. The nurse recognizes and treats hypoglycemia.

nursesNote: A patient on insulin becomes shaky, diaphoretic, and confused. The nurse confirms a blood glucose below 70 mg/dL and recognizes hypoglycemia — adrenergic signs (shakiness, sweating, palpitations, hunger, anxiety) and neuroglycopenic signs (confusion, irritability, slurred speech, seizures). For a conscious patient who can swallow, the nurse follows the Rule of 15: give 15 g of fast-acting carbohydrate (4 oz juice, glucose tablets/gel, or regular soda), recheck the glucose in 15 minutes, and repeat if still below 70; once normalized, provide a complex-carbohydrate snack or meal. High-fat foods (chocolate, nuts, peanut butter) are avoided initially because fat slows sugar absorption. If the patient is unconscious or cannot swallow safely, the nurse gives NOTHING by mouth and treats with IV dextrose (when access exists) or IM/SQ glucagon, then rechecks. Prevention teaching includes carrying a fast carb source and not skipping meals.
providerOrders: Confirm a blood glucose below 70 mg/dL and assess level of consciousness
Conscious patient: 15 g fast-acting carbohydrate, recheck in 15 minutes, repeat if needed
Follow normalized glucose with a complex-carbohydrate snack or meal
Unconscious or unable to swallow: nothing by mouth; give IV dextrose or glucagon
Teach prevention: carry fast carbs and avoid skipping meals

opts.text: Shakiness, sweating, and palpitations
Confusion, irritability, or slurred speech
A blood glucose below 70 mg/dL
Warm, dry, flushed skin with fruity breath
Sudden hunger and anxiety
Polyuria and polydipsia developing over days
Treat a conscious patient with 15 g of fast-acting carbohydrate
Recheck the blood glucose 15 minutes after treating
4 oz of juice or glucose tablets are appropriate fast carbs
Chocolate is the best first choice because fat speeds absorption
Give nothing by mouth if the patient is unconscious or cannot swallow
An unconscious patient needs IV dextrose or glucagon

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Conscious patient, BG 54, shaky and diaphoretic

opts.text: Confirm the low blood glucose
Give 15 g of fast-acting carbohydrate
Recheck the glucose in 15 minutes
Repeat the carbohydrate if still below 70
Once normalized, give a complex-carb snack or meal

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Classify each option for initial treatment.

opts.text: 4 oz of regular (non-diet) fruit juice
Glucose tablets (15 g)
4 oz of regular (non-diet) soda
A chocolate candy bar
A handful of nuts
1 tablespoon of honey or table sugar
A peanut butter sandwich
Glucose gel or hard candy

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Patient becomes unresponsive, cannot swallow, BG 38
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure airway and safety; give nothing by mouth
Call for help / activate rapid response per policy
Give IV dextrose if access, or IM/SQ glucagon
Recheck the blood glucose after treatment
When alert, give oral complex carbs and document
Teaching the patient to carry a fast-acting carb at home
Giving juice by mouth to a drowsy patient unsafe to swallow
Rechecking glucose 15 minutes after treating
Treating a confirmed BG of 55 with a high-fat snack first
Giving glucagon to an unconscious hypoglycemic patient
Over-treating with repeated large amounts of sugar
Following the low with a complex-carb snack once normalized
Delaying treatment to finish other tasks first
